subject: english
title: Unit 8 Opener
subtitle: That's Really Interesting! Hobbies, interests, and the Arctic photo.

content: Identify and describe hobbies and interests.
Write a caption for a photo.
language: Use related vocabulary: hobby, photographer, hiker, polar bear.
Use academic language: analyze, describe, explain, caption.
Discuss what makes a good photo caption.

slides.title: That's Really Interesting!
slides.teacherScript: Introduce Unit 8 as a photo-rich unit about hobbies, interests, and the Arctic.
slides.studentAction: Name one hobby or interesting photo topic.
bullets: Hobbies
Photography
Polar bears
Analyze and describe
slides.callout: Today Leo becomes a photo reporter.

slides.title: What We'll Learn Today
slides.teacherScript: Preview the unit and connect the opener to the rest of Unit 8.
slides.studentAction: Listen for the eight focus words and say which one feels most useful.
bullets: Identify and describe hobbies and interests.
Analyze a photo for information.
Write a caption for a photo.
Preview Grammar 1, Grammar 2, Reading, and Writing.
slides.callout: Focus words: hobby, photographer, hiker, polar bear, analyze, describe, explain, caption.

slides.title: hobby
slides.teacherScript: Ask Leo what he loves doing after school, then connect those activities to the word hobby.
slides.studentAction: Say three hobbies, then choose one favorite.
bullets: A hobby is something you love to do for fun in your free time.
Painting is a hobby.
Soccer can be Leo's hobby.
Wildlife photography can be a hobby.
slides.callout: Definition card needed in Reference.

slides.title: photographer
slides.teacherScript: Introduce Roie Galitz as a professional wildlife photographer.
slides.studentAction: Explain what a photographer does and why the Arctic photo is special.
bullets: A photographer is a person who takes photos.
A photographer can take photos as a hobby or as a job.
Roie Galitz is a professional wildlife photographer.
He traveled to the Arctic to photograph animals.
slides.callout: Definition card needed in Reference.

slides.title: hiker
slides.teacherScript: Connect hiking to reaching remote Arctic places.
slides.studentAction: Describe where a hiker might walk and what gear they might need.
bullets: A hiker walks long distances through nature.
Hikers may walk in mountains, forests, or wild areas.
Roie crossed Arctic terrain to reach polar bear territory.
Hiking can be exciting, tiring, and sometimes dangerous.
slides.callout: Definition card needed in Reference.

slides.title: polar bear
slides.teacherScript: Build interest in the hidden animal on the Arctic ice.
slides.studentAction: List three facts about polar bears.
bullets: A polar bear is a huge white bear that lives in the Arctic.
Polar bears can weigh hundreds of kilograms.
Polar bears are strong swimmers.
A curious polar bear found Roie's camera in Svalbard, Norway.
slides.callout: Definition card needed in Reference.

slides.title: Related Vocabulary Recap
slides.teacherScript: Point to each word and ask Leo for a fast definition before revealing support.
slides.studentAction: Define each word aloud: hobby, photographer, hiker, polar bear.
bullets: hobby: something you love to do for fun
photographer: a person who takes photos
hiker: someone who walks long distances through nature
polar bear: a huge white bear that lives in the Arctic
slides.callout: Use this as an oral check before academic language.

slides.title: analyze
slides.teacherScript: Explain that a reporter does not just look; a reporter analyzes to find information.
slides.studentAction: Look carefully at the photo and name five things that give information.
bullets: To analyze means to look carefully at something to understand it.
Analyze the photo; do not just look.
Think about what you see and what it tells you.
Use clues: animal, camera, ice, location, action.
slides.callout: Reference card linked.

slides.title: describe
slides.teacherScript: Model description with who, what, where, when, and how.
slides.studentAction: Describe the polar bear scene using at least three details.
bullets: To describe means to use words to tell what something looks like.
Description can tell where something is or what happened.
Use WHO, WHAT, WHERE, WHEN, and HOW.
Unit 8 goal: describe and explain a hobby.
slides.callout: Academic card needed in Reference.

slides.title: explain
slides.teacherScript: Contrast describe as WHAT with explain as WHY or HOW.
slides.studentAction: Explain why the polar bear may have found the camera.
bullets: To explain means to say why or how something happened.
Describe tells WHAT.
Explain tells WHY or HOW.
Give a reason, not only a detail.
slides.callout: Academic card needed in Reference.

slides.title: caption
slides.teacherScript: Show that a caption is short, specific, and interesting.
slides.studentAction: Choose the best caption and explain why it is best.
bullets: A caption is a short sentence under a photo.
A good caption tells who or what is in the photo.
A good caption says what is happening.
Best model: A curious polar bear investigates a photographer's camera in Svalbard, Norway.
slides.callout: Academic card needed in Reference.

slides.title: Academic Language Recap
slides.teacherScript: Check the difference between analyze, describe, explain, and caption.
slides.studentAction: Give one example of describe and one example of explain using the photo.
bullets: analyze: look carefully to find information
describe: tell what something looks like or what happened
explain: say why or how
caption: short sentence under a photo
slides.callout: Key difference: describe = WHAT; explain = WHY.

slides.title: Step Forward
slides.teacherScript: Read each activity. Leo steps forward when it sounds interesting.
slides.studentAction: Step forward for activities that sound interesting.
bullets: Playing soccer
Playing video games
Going to a museum
Fishing
Playing an instrument
Wildlife photography
Hiking in the Arctic
Drawing and painting
slides.callout: Use movement to make the opener active.

slides.title: Look at This Photo
slides.teacherScript: Ask Leo before showing model answers. Keep returning to evidence in the photo.
slides.studentAction: Answer the photo discussion questions.
bullets: What do you see in the photo?
What does the polar bear seem to be doing?
Where is the person who owns the camera?
How do you think the photographer felt?
slides.callout: Model answer: a curious polar bear is investigating a photographer's camera in Svalbard, Norway.

slides.title: About the Photo
slides.teacherScript: Use this background to enrich discussion, not as a lecture.
slides.studentAction: Ask one question about the photo, polar bears, or wildlife photography.
bullets: Photographer: Roie Galitz
Location: Svalbard / Greenland, Arctic
Context: wildlife photography workshop
The group backed away for safety as the bear came closer.
The bear investigated the camera equipment.
slides.callout: Discuss whether wildlife photography is a dangerous hobby.

slides.title: Encourage Curiosity
slides.teacherScript: If Leo becomes curious about polar bears, Greenland, or wildlife photography, encourage research.
slides.studentAction: Choose one topic to learn more about later.
bullets: Offer additional reading about polar bears or Arctic wildlife.
Use reliable sources such as National Geographic Kids, BBC Earth, or WWF.
Build vocabulary: Arctic, habitat, endangered, wildlife, photography.
Use curiosity to support active learning.
slides.callout: This is a teacher note, not a student quiz.

slides.title: Who Is Taking the Photo?
slides.teacherScript: Ask Leo to choose first, then discuss the funny double meaning.
slides.studentAction: Choose: a photographer, a hiker, or a polar bear.
bullets: Correct answer: a photographer.
Roie Galitz set up the camera.
Bonus idea: the polar bear is also involved because it investigates the camera.
Use this to connect photographer and polar bear.
slides.callout: Student Book p. 130.

slides.title: Write a Caption
slides.teacherScript: Have Leo write the caption in the Student Book or notebook.
slides.studentAction: Write one strong caption for the polar bear photo.
bullets: A great caption is specific.
A great caption is short.
A great caption uses vivid adjectives.
Useful words: curious, polar bear, photographer, investigates, Svalbard, Arctic, camera.
slides.callout: Good model: A curious polar bear investigates a photographer's camera in Svalbard, Norway.

slides.title: Formative Assessment
slides.teacherScript: Check both required Lesson Planner tasks before finishing.
slides.studentAction: Complete the oral vocabulary check and caption check.
bullets: Task 1: Leo identifies that a photographer took the photo.
Task 2: Leo writes a specific caption with at least one vivid adjective.
Vocabulary check: Leo defines all eight words.
Academic check: Leo explains describe = WHAT and explain = WHY.
slides.callout: The caption becomes Reporter's Notebook Entry #1.

slides.title: What's Next in Unit 8?
slides.teacherScript: Show how the opener connects to the rest of the unit.
slides.studentAction: Pick the next part of Unit 8 that sounds most interesting.
bullets: Vocabulary 1: collect, creative, enjoy, compete, cooperate, score
Vocabulary 2: bug, comic book, dinosaur, fossil, stuffed animal
Grammar 1: describing people with who
Grammar 2: direct and indirect objects
Reading: Hide and Seek
Writing: explanation writing
slides.callout: The Reporter's Notebook continues through the unit.

slides.title: Great Job, Leo!
slides.teacherScript: Celebrate completion and name the eight words one final time.
slides.studentAction: Say the eight words and choose the hardest one.
bullets: hobby
photographer
hiker
polar bear
analyze
describe
explain
caption
slides.callout: Reporter's Notebook Entry #1 is written.
